data.title: K-drama Love Me: Seo Hyun-jin leads slow-burn ensemble family drama with heavy emotions
data.description:
Lead cast: Seo Hyun-jin, Yoo Jae-myung, Chang Ryul
Latest Nielsen rating: 1.5 per cent
Loneliness, guilt and desire form the heart of Love Me, a melodrama that begins on a dour note before smoothing over the pain with the promise of new romance.
Although Seo Hyun-jin (The Trunk) gets the most screen time, this is at its core an ensemble piece about six characters: three members of a fractured family and the partners to which they slowly turn to mend their broken hearts.


This is the second series helmed by Jo Young-min this year, following the critically acclaimed Netflix drama You and Everything Else. The script is penned by writing duo Park Eun-young and Park Hee-kwon (I Have Not Done My Best Yet); Park Eun-young previously collaborated with Seo on The Trunk.
Seo stars as Seo Jun-kyung, an obstetrician-gynaecologist who has been living a lonely life filled with long shifts, after-work convenience store beers and disappointing blind dates ever since leaving her family home seven years earlier.
Her father, Seo Jin-ho (Yoo Jae-myung, Tempest), has just taken early retirement to better care for his physically disabled wife, Kim Mi-ran (Jang Hye-jin, Parasite). Still living at home is the college-age Seo Jun-seo (Lee Si-woo, Love Your Enemy).
Jun-kyung seldom returns home, and the mood is never festive when she does, as tensions quickly explode between mother and daughter.

The reason for these frosty relations is soon made clear: Mi-ran was incapacitated in a crash seven years ago while driving to Jun-kyung’s hospital on a rainy day to bring her something she had forgotten at home.
Jun-kyung’s guilt compelled her to leave home, and the family has never been able to repair the psychological damage caused by the accident.
Things go from bad to worse early on when tragedy strikes the family again. Yet from this low point, all that is left is for the remaining characters to rebuild, each in their own time and in their own way.
For Jun-kyung, this takes the form of her next-door neighbour Jo Du-hyun (Chang Ryul, Daily Dose of Sunshine), a free spirit who has quietly noticed her strength and hidden charms.

Jun-seo is already in a passionate relationship at the beginning of the series, but his girlfriend, a club entertainer, attracts a lot of attention, and jealousy starts to get the better of him.
Jun-seo’s friend, Ji Hye-on (Dahyun of K-pop group Twice), is quickly presented as an alternative love interest, though nothing sparks in the early episodes.
For Jin-ho, who has just become a widower, it may take longer to open up, but Jin Ja-young (Yoon Se-ah, Perfect Family) is cautiously introduced as a potential love interest in episode two.
With its weighty themes and their occasionally heavy-handed treatment, Love Me may not be everyone’s cup of tea. However, it largely achieves what it aims to, which is to say it succeeds as a tale of redemption, where romance is the saviour that pulls several characters back from the brink.

If the series suffers from any crucial flaw, it is the glaring age mismatch between some of its cast members. In real life, Seo is only 12 years younger than Yoo, who plays her father, and 10 years younger than Jang, who plays her mother, and the production does little to bridge that discrepancy.
Narrative television is make-believe, and actors are called upon to play characters of different ages, but suspension of disbelief can only take you so far. In Love Me, this creates a disconnect that at times makes it hard to get into the story.
Once we look past this issue, what we are left with is a show that is deliberate in its pacing, sensitive to the psychological needs of its characters, and honest about the flaws their wounds have woven into them.
The familial drama of the early episodes serves as a structural foundation from which three slow-burning romances can steadily rise. What is less clear yet is what kind of romantic series Love Me will turn into in the coming weeks.

Given the neediness of the title, there is a risk the narrative will focus squarely on the Seos and their emotional voids. As we learn more about their potential partners in the episodes ahead, hopefully the show will remember that love is a two-way street.
Love Me is streaming on Viu.

data.title: China vows city-specific fixes to clear housing glut, stabilise property sector
data.description:
China has pledged to empower local governments with city-specific measures to clear housing gluts and optimise supply next year, ramping up efforts to stabilise a real estate market that remains a persistent drag on the world’s second-largest economy.
“Municipal governments should fully utilise their autonomy in regulating the property market, adjusting and optimising policies as appropriate,” said Ni Hong, the minister of housing and urban-rural development, at a two-day work conference that ended on Tuesday.
While risk prevention remains the priority, the meeting served to emphasise that real estate “still has considerable development potential”, given the rising rate of urbanisation and people’s expectations for “quality homes”, according to an official readout.
To mitigate delivery risks in commercial housing, the ministry is pushing for a shift towards selling completed homes in a “what you see is what you get” model.
For projects that continue to operate on the traditional presales model – a key amplifier of the property crisis – Ni called for stricter oversight of funds to ensure that homebuyers’ rights are protected.
Authorities also pledged to support local governments in purchasing existing commercial housing for use as affordable units, resettlement housing and dormitories.
The ministry also declared that it had “fully accomplished” its campaign – enacted in 2022 – to revive stalled real estate projects and deliver new units in a timely manner.


The emphasis on selling finished homes marks a fundamental overhaul of the industry’s long-standing practice in which developers sold units long before completion, or sometimes before construction had begun. That business model unravelled in 2021 following the liquidity crisis of industry giant Evergrande, triggering a wave of developer defaults and stalled projects across the country.
Since then, China’s real estate sector, formerly a primary engine of economic growth, has been mired in a prolonged downturn. With considerable middle-class wealth tied up in depreciated properties, the slump has weighed heavily on consumption and reinforced deflationary pressures.
With 2026 marking the start of China’s 15th five-year planning period – which the ministry described as a “critical juncture of transformation and development” for housing – authorities are intensifying efforts to steady the sector.
However, some analysts warn that the decline will persist through next year.
China’s home prices continued to fall at a rapid pace in November, with tier-one cities, including Beijing, Guangzhou and Shenzhen, seeing new home prices fall 1.2 per cent from a year earlier, while resale home prices dropped 5.8 per cent.
The property sector is expected to produce a 1.5 percentage point drag on China’s GDP growth in 2026, according to a Goldman Sachs forecast on Friday.

data.title: Chinese start-ups Zhipu and MiniMax release latest AI models ahead of Hong Kong listing
data.description:
Chinese start-ups Zhipu AI and MiniMax have released new flagship artificial intelligence models within hours of each other, as they vie to become the world’s leading open-source AI systems supplier.
The near-simultaneous release by two of China’s “AI tigers” underscored efforts to drum up interest for their respective initial public offerings in Hong Kong, after both companies passed crucial regulatory hearings over the weekend.
Beijing-based Zhipu AI, known internationally as Z.ai, on Monday evening claimed that its new GLM-4.7 model features improved coding abilities, narrowing the gap with larger US firms Google DeepMind and Anthropic in innovative coding agents.
Zhipu AI touted the high scores achieved by GLM-4.7 on various coding benchmarks that surpassed those of OpenAI’s GPT-5, which was released in August. GLM-4.7’s scores were just behind those of Google DeepMind’s Gemini Pro 3 and Anthropic’s Claude Opus 4.5.
On SWE-Bench, a benchmark that evaluates models on real-world software engineering issues, Anthropic broke the 70 per cent mark in May via its older Claude Opus 4 model, becoming the first closed-source company to do so. Zhipu AI’s GLM-4.7 achieved the same feat for the first time on Monday.
In a blog post on the same day, Zhipu AI said benchmarks were “only one way to evaluate performance”. It encouraged users to get a “feel” for how the GLM-4.7 model worked.
Hours later, Shanghai-based MiniMax introduced its M2.1 model, which also featured marked improvements in coding abilities on internal tests, while similarly lagging behind Anthropic’s Claude Opus 4.5.
MiniMax claimed that M2.1 offered “industry-leading” capabilities to process different programming languages – including Java, Rust and Golang – at the same time.

The M2.1 also scored highly on a new in-house benchmark called VIBE, according to MiniMax. This benchmark evaluates AI models on the ability to “vibe code”, in which amateur programmers use generative AI to create custom software applications through language prompts.
While Zhipu AI has already released the weights of GLM-4.7, allowing developers around the world to deploy the model locally on their own hardware, MiniMax said it would release the weights of M2.1 later this week. A model’s weights are the variables encoding its “intelligence” that are adjusted during training.
GLM-4.7 was Zhipu AI’s third major update to its flagship model series in the past six months, while the new MiniMax release was a major update to the M2 model introduced just two months ago.
Alibaba Group Holding-backed Zhipu, founded in 2019 by professors at China’s prestigious Tsinghua University, could raise around US$300 million from its Hong Kong IPO next month, according to brokers. Alibaba owns the Post.
MiniMax, also backed by Alibaba, was founded in 2022 by former SenseTime computer vision expert Yan Junjie. The start-up was expected to raise as much as US$700 million from its Hong Kong listing, Reuters reported on Monday.
Both firms are expected to use their IPO proceeds to ramp up research and development spending, which would involve spending on advanced AI chips and data centre resources.
“The correlation between model quality and company survival is weakening [in China’s AI industry], while the correlation between capital access and survival is strengthening,” said Poe Zhao, a Beijing-based tech analyst and founder of the Hello China Tech newsletter.
According to its regulatory filing over the weekend, MiniMax revenue in the first nine months of 2025 grew nearly 175 per cent to US$53.4 million from a year earlier. Losses, meanwhile, widened 68 per cent to US$512 million.
Zhipu AI’s revenue jumped 325 per cent in the first half of 2025 to 190.9 million yuan (US$27 million), while losses climbed to 2.36 billion yuan.

data.title: Committee probing Hong Kong fire visits Wang Fuk Court to inspect conditions
data.description:
Members of an independent committee created to investigate Hong Kong’s deadliest fire in decades inspected the scene of the blaze on Tuesday morning to get a better understanding of the actual environment and conditions at Wang Fuk Court.
Judge David Lok Kai-hong, the group’s chairman, was joined by fellow committee members such as Executive Council non-official member Chan Kin-por and outgoing MTR Corporation chairman Rex Auyeung Pak-kuen.
Committee members were spotted wearing full protective gowns as they arrived at the housing complex in Tai Po at about 11am. They inspected Wang Chi House, the only tower among the eight at Wang Fuk Court to be undamaged by the inferno, before leaving at around 12.20pm.
In a statement issued by the committee on Tuesday afternoon, the body said it had inspected various indoor and outdoor locations at the estate, without specifying which areas.
“[The inspection aimed] to gain a better understanding of the actual environment and conditions of the estate; the causes and circumstances that led to the fire and its rapid spread; the fire service installations and equipment used in the buildings; the materials used in building maintenance and renovation works; and construction safety matters,” it said.
The statement included a photo showing Lok, Chan and Auyeung listening to briefings held at the scene. It is understood that the Fire Services Department conducted parts of the briefings.
The committee convened its first meeting last Friday, with chairman Lok promising that it would do “its utmost in discharging its duties” and aimed to complete its work within nine months before submitting a report to the city’s leader.
The catastrophic 43-hour blaze at Wang Fuk Court started on November 26 and claimed 161 lives, including firefighter Ho Wai-ho. Nearly 5,000 residents were also displaced by the fire.
The estate had been undergoing renovations since July last year and was covered in scaffolding and mesh. Some scaffold net samples collected in the aftermath failed fire-retardant tests.




Chief Executive John Lee Ka-chiu ordered the creation of the committee in early December to discover the cause of the blaze and initiate “systemic reforms”.
The committee has no legal power to compel and protect witnesses, but can apply to the city leader to cut red tape and receive judicial powers to ensure the investigation is completed within nine months.
All findings are set to be made public.
The city leader named eight areas for the committee to focus on, including checking existing safety requirements, supervision and routine maintenance for construction works, as well as the level of supervision and accountability related to the installation and operation of fire safety systems.
The committee is expected to create an updated list of clearly defined safety standards for construction materials, while uncovering systemic issues within the building process, including potential collusion and conflicts of interest.
It will also examine the role and responsibilities of regulatory personnel across all stages of Wang Fuk Court’s renovation works, including government departments and authorised professionals.
Examining corruption, bid-rigging and irregular tender practices within the building maintenance sector also falls within the committee’s scope, with the body tasked with evaluating the adequacy of existing legislation and penalties to ensure proper accountability.
Authorities have so far arrested at least 23 people on suspicion of offences such as manslaughter and corruption in connection with the fire.

data.title: Australian policeman injured in Bondi terror attack wakes from coma
data.description:
A police officer injured in the Bondi terror attack at a Hanukkah celebration in Australia has woken from a medically induced coma, his family said.
New South Wales (NSW) police constable Scott Dyson, 25, was one of two officers wounded when investigators allege Naveed Akram and his father Sajid opened fire on a crowd of more than 1,000 people at Sydney’s Archer Park on December 14.
The attack killed 15 people and injured dozens more.
Accused gunman Naveed Akram, 24, faces 15 murder charges and one terrorism charge, while his father, 50, was shot dead by police at the scene.
In a statement released on Tuesday, Dyson’s family said their son has been in an induced coma and undergone surgery “almost daily”.


“Our family would like to share that Scott has been getting stronger each day, and today, he was woken up for the first time,” they said.
“There is still a long way to go in his recovery, but this is a positive sign.
“We would like to thank the public for the support, messages and well wishes shown to Scott and our family during this difficult time.”
The family of probationary constable Jack Hibbert said on Tuesday he had been discharged from hospital.
“While he is home, he is still recovering and will need space, support and continued positive thoughts during this time,” they said in a statement.
“As a family, we couldn’t ask for anything more – having our Jack home, especially for Christmas, truly feels like a miracle.”

NSW Health on Tuesday said 12 patients remained in hospital receiving care in the aftermath of the attack.
Four patients are in a stable but critical condition.
Naveed Akram is also accused of 40 counts of attempted murder, discharging a firearm intending to cause grievous bodily harm, a public display of a prohibited terrorist organisation symbol and placing an explosive in or near a building with intent to cause harm.
He was transferred from hospital to prison on Monday and will face a Sydney court again in the new year.
He has yet to enter a plea over the charges.
The massacre at Bondi – Australia’s worst in almost three decades – prompted an international outcry and government plans to tighten the country’s gun laws and hate-speech regulations.

On Tuesday, New South Wales, Australia’s most populous state and where the shooting took place, was set to approve sweeping laws cracking down on guns and giving authorities the power to ban protests.
Facing growing political pressure over the December 14 attack, state and federal governments have proposed changes to gun laws and a broad hate speech ban.
The NSW government has recalled its parliament to introduce what it called the “toughest firearm reforms in the country”.
The new rules will cap the number of guns an individual can own to four, or 10 for exempted individuals like farmers.
The legislation will also ban the display of “terrorist symbols”, including the flag of Islamic State.
And it will give authorities power to prohibit protests for up to three months following a terrorist incident.
The reforms are expected to pass the upper house of the New South Wales parliament on Tuesday evening or early Wednesday.

State Premier Chris Minns said the laws will “keep the people of New South Wales safe”.
“Whether that’s on gun regulation in New South Wales or, secondly, changes to protest, in order to lower the temperature in Sydney,” he told reporters.
A broad coalition of groups has vowed a constitutional legal challenge to the anti-protest laws.
Palestine Action Group Sydney, one of those involved in the challenge, accused the state of having “pushed through legislation without due process, attacking our fundamental right to protest”.
It also accused the state of making “unsubstantiated and plainly dishonest links between antisemitism and the Palestine solidarity movement”.
Australia’s federal government is at the same time pushing for new laws creating an aggravated offence for hate preaching, penalties for those deemed to have sought to radicalise minors and a new register of allegedly extremist groups it will become illegal to join.
It will also pay gun owners to surrender “surplus, newly banned and illegal firearms”.
It would be the largest gun buy-back since 1996, when Australia cracked down on firearms in the wake of a shooting that killed 35 people at Port Arthur.
Additional reporting by Reuters

data.title: 3 arrested over use of fake surveying equipment certificates in Hong Kong projects
data.description:
Hong Kong police have arrested three people linked to a company suspected of using surveying equipment with forged certificates in at least two redevelopment projects and a public hospital expansion, the Post has learned.
The arrests, involving acoustic and environmental services consultant Novox, were made on Tuesday.
The Urban Renewal Authority (URA) told the Post that Novox provided surveying equipment to four of its projects in To Kwa Wan, with two confirming the use of instruments with forged certificates.
A source said three individuals linked to Novox were arrested on suspicion of using fake instruments. If convicted, the maximum penalty is 14 years’ imprisonment.
The two projects, located at Bailey Street/Wing Kwong Street and Hung Fook Street/Ngan Hon Street, were tendered to Nation Star Development and Port Destiny, respectively.
Nation Star is owned by Henderson Land Development while Port Destiny is under Kerry Properties.
A URA spokesman said that after the developers confirmed the use of forged certificates, the authority had sent letters requesting them to “immediately terminate their contracts with Novox and suggesting they file police reports.”
He said the URA also told the developers of the other two projects to stop using the instruments and terminate their contracts with Novox, even if they had yet to confirm the authenticity of the certificates.
All sites have since hired a new subcontractor for surveying services with no impact on work progress, he said.
The URA has further urged developers and contractors to verify subcontractors’ instrument certification documents and strengthen project oversight.
Novox was also a subcontractor on a URA management project supplying noise and particulate monitors, and the authority has told the main contractor to terminate its contract with the company.
According to its website, Novox, founded in 2018, provides acoustic and environmental consultancy services for projects from design and planning to construction and component testing.
The URA said the surveying equipment was used to monitor air quality and noise levels, which was not legally required and had no direct bearing on construction standards or safety.
Novox also reportedly provided environmental monitoring services for the airport’s Terminal 2 expansion.
In a reply to the Post, the Airport Authority stopped short of mentioning the company involved but said it had terminated the work.
The authority said it reviewed the certificates of its environmental monitoring instruments with its main contractor, and the accreditation body verified their issuance.
Earlier this month, the Hospital Authority reported to police an allegedly fake equipment certificate submitted by Novox for its Princess Margaret Hospital expansion in Kwai Chung.

data.title: China’s Xiaomi rejects claims of military ties after US lawmakers’ appeal to Pentagon
data.description:
Chinese tech giant Xiaomi on Tuesday rejected calls by a group of US lawmakers to add it to a Pentagon list of companies alleged to have links with China’s military, describing the move as baseless and reiterating its focus on the consumer market.
The smartphone-to-vehicle giant said it was “not a Chinese military company nor affiliated with any Chinese military entities” and that it “has always been and continues to be a consumer product company”.
“[Xiaomi] provides products and services for civilian and commercial use only,” the company said in a statement on Tuesday. “There is no basis for the inclusion of Xiaomi on the 1260H list.”
The rebuttal followed an open letter last week in which nine Republican lawmakers, including several congressional committee chairs, urged US Defence Secretary Pete Hegseth to add more than a dozen Chinese technology firms to the Pentagon’s Section 1260H list. The others included artificial intelligence start-up DeepSeek and humanoid robot maker Unitree.
DeepSeek, Unitree and the Pentagon did not immediately respond to a request for comment on Tuesday.
Unlike a trade blacklist that triggers immediate sanctions, the 1260H list designates companies the Pentagon claims have ties to China’s military and serves as a warning to US entities and government agencies about the risks of doing business with them.
The lawmakers urged the inclusion of a range of other Chinese firms spanning biotech, life sciences manufacturing, robotics and embodied AI, display makers and chip developers.
Some Chinese companies, including drone maker DJI, have previously sued the US Defense Department over their inclusion.
Beijing-based Xiaomi was placed on the US Defense Department’s “Communist Chinese Military Company” (CCMC) list in early 2021. That list was later phased out and replaced by the 1260H list. Xiaomi swiftly filed a lawsuit challenging the designation and was subsequently removed from the CCMC list that year.

data.title: Tours to visit Christmas movie locations in US state of Connecticut prove huge hit
data.description:
Christmas at Pemberley Manor and Romance at Reindeer Lodge may never make it to Oscar night, but legions of fans still love these sweet-yet-predictable holiday movies – and this season, many are making pilgrimages to where their favourite scenes were filmed.
That is because the US state of Connecticut – the location for at least 22 holiday films by American cable networks such as Hallmark and Lifetime – has been promoting tours of the quaint Christmas-card cities and towns featured in this booming film market. Places where, for example, a busy corporate lawyer can return home for the holidays and cross paths with a plaid-shirt-clad former high school flame who now runs a Christmas tree farm. (Spoiler alert: they live happily ever after.)
“It’s exciting – just to know that something was in a movie and we actually get to see it visually,” said Abby Rumfelt of Morganton, North Carolina, after stepping off a coach in Wethersfield, Connecticut, one of the stops on the holiday film tour.
Rumfelt was among 53 people, mostly women, on a recent week-long “Hallmark Movie Christmas Tour”, organised by Mayfield Tours from Spartanburg, South Carolina. On the bus, fans watched the matching film as they rode from stop to stop.

Whether you love them or loathe them, television Christmas films are a holiday season staple. The romance, the snowy landscapes, the perfectly decorated homes and the familiar stories provide millions of viewers with comfort and joy in December – and even November for some with a looser definition of the holiday season.
Of course, some of that comforting familiarity comes from the conventions that are now expected in a network Christmas film – for example, the idea of reconnecting with a school sweetheart or the presence of an older, bearded gentleman who may or may not be Santa Claus.
Hallmark holiday films have reached 24 million people so far this season and Lifetime saw more than 23 million people tune in last year, according to data from Nielsen.
It is no surprise these tours have been a hit. To plan one, Mayfield Tours co-owner Debbie Mayfield used the “Connecticut Christmas Movie Trail” map, which was launched by the New England state last year to cash in on the growing Christmas film craze.
Mayfield, who co-owns the company with her husband, Ken, said this was their first Christmas tour to holiday film locations in Connecticut and other Northeastern states. It included hotel accommodation, some meals, tickets and even a stop to see the Rockettes in New York City. It sold out in two weeks.
With snow in the air and Christmas songs piped from a speaker, the group stopped for lunch at Heirloom Market at Comstock Ferre, where parts of the Hallmark films Christmas on Honeysuckle Lane and Rediscovering Christmas were filmed.

Once home to America’s oldest seed company, the store is located in a historical district known for its stately 1700s and 1800s buildings. It is an ideal setting for a holiday film. Even the local country store has sold T-shirts featuring Hallmark’s crown logo and the phrase “I Live in a Christmas Movie. Wethersfield, CT 06109.”
“People just know about us now,” said Julia Koulouris, who co-owns the market with her husband, Spiros, crediting the film trail in part. “And you see these things on Instagram and stuff where people are tagging it and posting it.”
The concept of holiday films dates back to the 1940s, when Hollywood produced classics like It’s A Wonderful Life, Miracle on 34th Street and Christmas in Connecticut, which was actually shot at the Warner Bros studios in Burbank, California.
In 2006, five years after the launch of the Hallmark Channel on TV, Hallmark “struck gold” with the romance film The Christmas Card, said Joanna Wilson, author of the book Tis the Season TV: The Encyclopedia of Christmas-Themed Episodes, Specials and Made-for-TV Movies.
“Hallmark saw those high ratings and then started creating that format and that formula with the tropes and it now has become their dominant formula that they create for their Christmas TV romances,” she said.

The holiday film industry, estimated to generate hundreds of millions of dollars a year, has expanded beyond Hallmark and Lifetime.
Today, a mix of cable and broadcast networks, streaming platforms and direct-to-video producers release roughly 100 new films annually, Wilson said. The genre has also diversified, with characters from a wider range of racial and ethnic backgrounds as well as LGBTQ storylines.
The formula, however, remains the same. And fans still have an appetite for a family-friendly love story.
“They want to see people coming together. They want to see these romances. It’s a part of the hope of the season,” Wilson said. “Who doesn’t love love? And it always has a predictable, happy ending.”
Hazel Duncan, 83, of Forest City, North Carolina, said she and her husband of 65 years, Owen, like to watch the films together year-round because they are sweet and family-friendly. They also take her back to their early years as a young couple, when life felt simpler.
“We hold hands sometimes,” she said. “It’s kind of sweet. We’ve got two recliners back in a bedroom that’s real small and we’ve got the TV there. And we close the doors off and it’s just our time together in the evening.”

Connecticut’s chief marketing officer, Anthony M. Anthony, said the Christmas Movie Trail is part of a multipronged rebranding effort launched in 2023 that promotes the state not just as a tourist destination, but also as a place to work and live.
“So what better way to highlight our communities as a place to call home than them being sets of movies?” he said.
However, there continues to be debate at the state Capitol over whether to eliminate or cap film industry tax credits – which could threaten how many more of these films will be made locally.
Christina Nieves and her husband of 30 years, Raul, already live in Connecticut and have been tackling the trail “little by little”.
It had been a chance, she said, to explore new places in the state, like the Bushnell Park Carousel in Hartford, where a scene from Ghost of Christmas Always was filmed.

It also inspired Nieves to convince her husband – not quite the film fan she is – to join her at a tree lighting and Christmas parade in their hometown of Windsor Locks.
“I said, listen, let me just milk this Hallmark thing as long as I can, OK?” she said.
Additional reporting by Tribune News Service

data.title: Ben Ainslie strikes Oakley Capital deal to keep British America’s Cup bid afloat
data.description:
British ‌sailor Ben Ainslie has finally secured the backing he needs for his 2027 America’s Cup campaign, announcing a deal ‍with Oakley Capital on Tuesday after what he admitted has been a stressful chapter in his mission to “bring the Cup home”.
Ainslie’s Athena Racing team said a majority ownership investment from the British private equity group would provide “long-term financial stability to compete not only in the next America’s Cup but across multiple future cycles”.
“Up ⁠until now, basically, I’ve been funding the team myself … yeah, I’d say it’s been pretty stressful. But I believed in the team, I believed in the [America’s Cup] partnership, and I was willing to take that risk. So I’m delighted that that’s been worthwhile,” Ainslie said.
As well as being the most successful Olympic sailor, the 48-year-old also lifted the “Auld Mug” in 2013 with Oracle Team USA and has made it his ‍goal to secure a first victory for Britain, where the event was first contested off the south coast in 1851.
However, Ainslie was left ‍without funding for his latest campaign to win the coveted trophy after parting ways in January with INEOS, which had financed his attempts in 2021 and 2024.

Athena Racing said Oakley will help them to take ‌full advantage of the newly formed America’s Cup Partnership (ACP), which has been backed by five teams and will mean the event is now held every ‍two years, raising its visibility for sponsors.
“The America’s Cup is the pinnacle of sailing and an icon of sport, won by only four nations since 1851,” Peter Dubens, Oakley’s co-founder and managing partner, said.
“With strong governance and a neutral management team focused on audience and media growth, the competition is set to broaden its appeal, improve accessibility and ‌ensure long-term ⁠sustainability.”
Ainslie, who will remain team principal, said Oakley’s investment would give Britain all the financial firepower needed to challenge defenders New Zealand in Naples, Italy, in 2027.
The new ACP framework imposes a spending cap of €75 million (US$88 million) for each team’s AC38 campaign, then €55 million for subsequent events.
While the Oakley deal opened the door to sponsorship, Ainslie said he did ‌not expect to announce anything imminently.
“We’ve been having some positive conversations with various brands … The agencies that we’ve been talking to that are supporting us with the sponsorship search are really bullish and positive about what we’re selling,” Ainslie added.
The British ‌team will get a rebrand and ‌the boat they used to challenge New Zealand last year is already in transit from Barcelona to Naples where it will undergo modifications.
Ainslie said the team and their boat would have a very British feel and focus, adding that having this association with the country is an important feature for the America’s Cup.
“We are really trying to come up with something innovative and exciting for all the supporters out there,” he said.

data.title: Alibaba’s DingTalk rolls out Agent OS as it bets bigger on AI agents
data.description:
DingTalk, the workplace collaboration app developed by Alibaba Group Holding, has unveiled a dedicated operating system and new enterprise hardware as it steps up its push into AI agents amid an intensifying arms race among China’s tech giants.
At an event in Hangzhou on Tuesday, the Alibaba unit launched Agent OS, an operating system designed specifically for AI agents, alongside DingTalk Real, a hardware suite that serves as a physical terminal for running those agents. Alibaba owns the South China Morning Post.
“Moving forward, all AI agents on DingTalk will be built and run on Agent OS, allowing AI to connect directly with the physical world,” Chen Hang, founder and chief executive of DingTalk, said at the event.
AI agents – such as those developed by Chinese start-up Butterfly Effect’s Manus AI – are software programmes capable of autonomously planning and executing tasks on behalf of users or systems, breaking down complex goals into specific actions using available resources.
The move underscores DingTalk’s ambition to evolve from a pure software platform into a full-stack provider of AI agent technologies, combining both hardware and software for enterprise customers.
The push comes as Alibaba competes more aggressively with domestic rivals including Tencent Holdings and ByteDance, which are also accelerating their investments in AI agents.

Chinese Big Tech firms have sharpened their focus on the segment following the overseas relocation of Manus AI, a high-profile AI agent project founded by Chinese entrepreneurs, amid rising geopolitical tensions.
Chen described DingTalk Real as the “body of AI”, designed to enable agents to operate “securely and reliably” within complex corporate environments.
He emphasised the company’s focus on safety, noting that the hardware allowed users to halt operations by physically cutting power if an AI agent behaves unpredictably.
DingTalk also introduced a specialised “homework correction machine” aimed at educational use, capable of grading up to 100 assignments in four minutes. Targeting primary and middle schools, the device is available for pre-orders, with an initial roll-out planned for 1,000 schools.
On the software side, DingTalk rolled out a suite of workplace AI agents that automate tasks such as recruitment, travel expense claims and marketing design.
The AI Travel agent, for instance, could plan itineraries, compare prices and file expense claim requests automatically on behalf of users, according to Chen.
Chen founded DingTalk in 2015 before leaving Alibaba six years later to pursue his own venture. He returned earlier this year as chief executive, as Alibaba ramped up its AI strategy across its broader ecosystem.
